question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the policy for future consideration of temporary release; and (ii) the factors which are taken into consideration, in the event of a prisoner absconding from temporary release.
answer_text: Prison Rule 27 gives the Department of Justice a discretionary power to release certain prisoners temporarily for any period or periods and subject to any conditions.  This power is exercised by the Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) on behalf of the Department.   This rule applies to prisoners other than those remanded in custody by any court; those committed to custody for trial; or those committed to be sentenced or otherwise dealt with before or by the Crown Court.   The Prison Service uses Rule 27 to grant qualifying prisoners temporary release from prison for a number of reasons, for example, for pre-release testing commissioned by the Parole Commissioners; for compassionate temporary release to attend a funeral or to visit a critically ill close family member; or to attend hospital appointments. Before a prisoner leaves custody under Rule 27 they are subject to a risk assessment.  They will also be made subject to a number of conditions designed to regulate and control their behaviour while they are in the community.   In the event a prisoner absconds while on temporary release they may be made subject to the Prison Service’s adjudication process on their return to custody.  They may also be subject to prosecution under section 25 of the Prison Act (NI) 1953 which creates an offence of “being unlawfully at large while under sentence” with a conviction resulting in the offender being liable to imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years. An incident of absconding will also be taken into account when any future temporary release is being considered as part of a risk assessment process.It should be noted that, where this takes place as part of the pre-release testing regime, Prison Service are under an obligation to resume pre-release testing after a short period.  This is one of the elements which I have asked the Director General to review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the policy for future consideration of temporary release; and (ii) what factors are taken into consideration, in the event of a prisoner absconding from temporary release, granted on compassionate grounds.
answer_text: Prison Rule 27 gives the Department of Justice a discretionary power to release certain prisoners temporarily for any period or periods and subject to any conditions.  This power is exercised by the Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) on behalf of the Department.   This rule applies to prisoners other than those remanded in custody by any court; those committed to custody for trial; or those committed to be sentenced or otherwise dealt with before or by the Crown Court.   The Prison Service uses Rule 27 to grant qualifying prisoners temporary release from prison for a number of reasons, for example, for pre-release testing commissioned by the Parole Commissioners; for compassionate temporary release to attend a funeral or to visit a critically ill close family member; or to attend hospital appointments. Before a prisoner leaves custody under Rule 27 they are subject to a risk assessment.  They will also be made subject to a number of conditions designed to regulate and control their behaviour while they are in the community.   In the event a prisoner absconds while on compassionate temporary release they may be made subject to the Prison Service’s adjudication process on their return to custody.  They may also be subject to prosecution under section 25 of the Prison Act (NI) 1953 which creates an offence of “being unlawfully at large while under sentence” with a conviction resulting in the offender being liable to imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years. An incident of absconding while on compassionate temporary release will also be taken into account when any future temporary release is being considered as part of a risk assessment process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the policy for the temporary release of prisoners prior to (a) final release; and (b) release on compassionate grounds; and (ii) what factors are taken into consideration.
answer_text: Prison Rule 27 gives the Department of Justice a discretionary power to release certain prisoners temporarily for any period or periods and subject to any conditions.  This power is exercised by the Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) on behalf of the Department.   This rule applies to prisoners other than those remanded in custody by any court; those committed to custody for trial; or those committed to be sentenced or otherwise dealt with before or by the Crown Court.   The Prison Service uses Rule 27 to grant qualifying prisoners temporary release from prison for a number of reasons, for example, for pre-release testing commissioned by the Parole Commissioners; for compassionate temporary release to attend a funeral or to visit a critically ill close family member; or to attend hospital appointments. Prisoners leaving custody under Rule 27 are subject to a risk assessment.  They are also subject to a number of conditions designed to regulate and control their behaviour while they are in the community.   Whilst the majority of prisoners comply with the conditions of their release, there are some who do not, and this is a cause of concern. I have, therefore, asked the Director General to review the management of prisoners, with a view to reducing risk, and await her findings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she intends to review the policies around prisoner temporary release.
answer_text: Following the recent return of a prisoner who absconded during a period of pre-release testing, I have asked the Northern Ireland Prison Service to review what further steps could be taken to improve the management of risk of those seeking temporary release or who are undergoing periods of pre-release testing.  This will include looking at what can be done to strengthen the risk management for these individuals, and the sanctions they will face if they go unlawfully at large in future.  That work is in progress and I plan to provide further information once it is complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the regional system for breast cancer referrals, announced in May 2025, is delivering the desired improvements.
To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any scrutiny that applies to the formulation, amendment or application of the Injury Benefits Scheme rules.
answer_text: The Civil Service Injury Benefit Scheme is established in law under Article 3 of the Superannuation (Northern Ireland) Order 1972. The current formulation of the rules reflects the rules of the equivalent scheme in Britain. Under Article 3(2) of the 1972 Order the Department of Finance is required to consult with ‘representatives of those likely to be affected’ before any amendment is made to the rules. As part of this process Trade Unions representing the relevant employees are provided with an opportunity to scrutinise any proposed amendments.The rules of the scheme are administered by Civil Service Pensions Branch. In line with the requirements of the Pensions (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 if any individual is dissatisfied with how the scheme rules are applied to their case they may appeal the outcome using the Civil Service Pensions dispute resolution process. If they remain dissatisfied with the outcome of this review process they may make an appeal to the Pensions Ombudsman to further scrutinise the scheme’s decision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail whether any conflicts have been identified or addressed in relation to notional payments under the Injury Benefits Scheme.
answer_text: The application of a notional deduction under the relevant rules of the Injury Benefit Scheme has been raised with the Department in correspondence, and also via Industrial Tribunal. The Department has contended that such deductions were lawfully and reasonably applied under the rules of the scheme.  There have been no challenges brought against the scheme which have resulted in any ruling or requirement that the Department address or change the rules in relation to its legal authority to apply a notional deduction in respect of the range of state benefits currently specified under rule 1.8 of the Injury Benefits Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether the Injury Benefit Scheme rules have the force of law or whether they are considered guidance or policy.
answer_text: The rules of the Civil Service Injury Benefit Scheme are established in secondary legislation entitled the ‘Civil Service Injury Benefits Scheme (Northern Ireland) 2003’, which is made under authority and vires of a provision in primary legislation, namely the ‘Superannuation (Northern Ireland) Act 1972’. The scheme rules are law and not guidance or policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to clarify (i) the use of notional payments under the Injury Benefits Scheme; (ii) whether these are set out in legislation; (iii) where the rules governing the Injury Benefits Scheme and the use of notional payments are published; and (iv) how they are made accessible to affected individuals.
answer_text: (i) The rules of the Civil Service Injury Benefit Scheme require that an award made for injury related loss of earnings must take account of any other public-funded state benefits to which an individual may have an entitlement. The initial calculation of an injury benefit award may therefore include a ‘notional’ deduction in respect of an individual’s actual or likely entitlement to any of the state benefits currently specified at rule 1.8 (iii) of the Injury Benefit Scheme rules. When the claimant has tested their entitlement for that benefit with the Department for Communities and the relevant benefit amount is confirmed the injury award will be readjusted accordingly. (ii) The legislation for the rules of the Civil Service Injury Benefit Scheme is the ‘Civil Service Injury Benefits Scheme (Northern Ireland) 2003’ which is made under Article 3 of the Superannuation (Northern Ireland) Act 1972. (iii) The current rules and associated guidance are published on the Department of Finance website at:https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/publications/civil-service-injury-benefits-scheme-ni-2003https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/publications/injury-benefits-scheme-brief-guide(iv) The rules and associated guidance can be accessed on the Department’s website. Affected individuals will also receive further information, including on the process involving notional deductions, in the injury award application pack which all claimants are provided with.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on her plans to address the findings and recommendations contained in the Criminal Justice Inspection Northern Ireland report, Transforming the Criminal Justice System in Northern Ireland - A Strategic Overview.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 9 – AQO 2221/22-27			   ANSWER I welcome the report by the Criminal Justice Inspection Northern Ireland published on 28 November 2024, which provides a strategic overview of transformation in Northern Ireland’s criminal justice system.  CJINI plays a key role in Northern Ireland’s criminal justice system, inspecting and identifying areas for improvements. Each report is considered carefully by the relevant justice agencies and over the years many of the recommendations have been implemented within the system.   This inspection looked at how criminal justice partners worked together with the Department of Justice to secure transformation and highlight the challenges experienced.  In their report, CJINI made three recommendations, The department has confirmed that the strategic recommendation, which relates to agreement of a shared future vision and strategic priorities across the criminal justice, is the responsibility of the Department and work is ongoing.  At present work is at an advanced stage to outline a Digital Justice Strategy which covers all of justice – Criminal, Civil and Family and Tribunals. Work is also progressing on a separate Vision which will focus on Criminal Justice.  The first operational recommendation was rejected by PSNI as this work was already being taken forward.  The Department of Justice in conjunction with all criminal justice organisations (CJO’s) have responsibility for taking forward the second operational recommendation and this has also been actioned.   There have been a number of improvements since the devolution of policing and justice matters.  However, there is a need for continuous reform due to the ever-changing operating environment in which we exist.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any action she is taking to prevent further closures within the retail, hospitality and small business sectors.
answer_text: Our retail and hospitality sectors have displayed remarkable resilience in emerging from the pandemic and adapting to rising costs. I’m fully aware concerns have been raised surrounding the tax and spending changes implemented in early April as announced in the British Government’s Autumn Statement. In my previous role as Finance Minister, I commissioned the Ulster University Economic Policy Centre to examine the Cost of Doing Business in the north. The research was recently published, and I, along with the current Finance Minister, will be using this as evidence to further advocate for taxation changes to support our local businesses. Minister O’Dowd raised the issues with employer’s national insurance contributions and VAT with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury at the Finance Interministerial Standing Committee in February. I will continue to work closely with the Minister of Finance in lobbying for changes to support our businesses.My Department along with Invest NI and Tourism NI provide a broad range of support to businesses to help them be more competitive through energy efficiency, innovation, skills, and exploring new markets. I am also committed to engaging with the local retail and hospitality sectors to understand the issues they are facing. In the past week I have met with Retail NI to discuss the cost of doing business.There are several ways the Executive already provides support to the retail, hospitality and small business sectors. Land and Property Services within the Department of Finance offer a wide range of rate support schemes worth a total of £275m to support business ratepayers, alongside offering alternative payment options to assist those with rating debt.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail all areas of cross-departmental work which are being delayed because an agreed lead has not been identified.
answer_text: It has not been possible to agree an appropriate lead for the actions within the Strategic Priority of ‘Social Care’ within the Improving Health within Criminal Justice Strategy (“Strategy”).  Social care is a broad term which refers to the support and assistance provided to people who need extra help to live independently, safely, and with dignity.  It includes areas such as support with daily living; promoting independence; and emotional and social wellbeing. At the time of publishing the Strategy, it was acknowledged that further work was required to agree a joint departmental position on where responsibility for social care resided.In order to progress the issue, the Department of Health set up a multi-agency Task and Finish Group in 2022.  This Group worked up a proposal for the provision of a personal care service across the three prison sites.  However, it was not progressed as it was not deemed to be value for money by either Department.  More recently however, the Department of Health has commissioned a scoping exercise on the social care needs within the prisons.  This work is nearing completion and will be used to inform renewed discussions across both Departments as to what additional services are required, how they can be provided as cost-effectively as possible and where responsibility resides.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail all areas of cross-departmental work with her Department which are being delayed as a result of failure to identify an agreed lead.
answer_text: My department is involved in many areas of work with other Executive departments.  There are currently no areas of cross-departmental work which are being delayed as a result of a failure to identify an agreed lead.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail what (i) reassurances; and (ii) advice, she can give to those prison officers who remain concerned about their information having been potentially compromised, as a result of the break-in in Dundonald House in March 2025.
answer_text: The incidents of unauthorised access to Dundonald House were reported to the PSNI and that, while the data remained secure and uncompromised, immediate steps were taken to further secure the building and relocate all files to a secure location.  The Director General wrote to staff on 24 March and 1 April to ensure they were aware of the actions taken in response to the incidents and to provide assurance that no sensitive or personal information had been compromised.  Trade Union colleagues were regularly updated on the incidents and were also given the opportunity to visit Dundonald House to provide assurance that no breach of information had occurred and to witness the steps being taken to relocate all files. I would encourage prison officers to ensure they have read the notes issued to all staff from the Director General regarding this issue which provided the reassurance that no information has been compromised.  If required, staff can also discuss this with their managers or Trade Union representative.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the nature of any documentation remaining in Dundonald House relating to (i) her Department; and specifically (ii) the Northern Ireland Prison Service, at the time of the break-in in March 2025.
answer_text: At the time of the unauthorised access to Dundonald House in March 2025, there were personnel, policy and operational files relating to the Northern Ireland Prison Service.  These files were stored within secure filing cabinets in locked rooms and were not accessed by the intruders.  Following the unauthorised access, the Prison Service immediately relocated all relevant files.  No Prison Service documentation, confidential or otherwise, remains within Dundonald House.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail all areas of cross-departmental work with her Department which are being delayed as a result of failure to identify an agreed lead.
answer_text: It has not been possible to date to identify an appropriate lead Department for the provision of social care in prisons.  However, the Department of Health has commissioned a needs assessment of social care provision within prisons and it is anticipated that the final report, when available, will inform the way forward.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any consideration they will give to reduce the number of departmental strategies produced to avoid duplication and siloed department working and ensure a more focussed and efficient implementation of strategies.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what action her Department can take to avoid the closure of Belfast Metropolitan's Castlereagh Campus.
answer_text: Belfast Metropolitan College is currently working through the business case process to consider all options for the future of its Castlereagh Campus in line with its responsibilities for estate management.  Colleges are obligated to submit decisions of this nature to the Department for my consideration and approval. I remain fully committed to ensuring the Further Education estate is fit for purpose for the delivery of a high-quality curriculum and to ensure it will be financially sustainable into the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what discussions she has had with Belfast Metropolitan College regarding (i) the proposed closure; (ii) the consultation responses; and (iii) options to secure the campus' future.
answer_text: Belfast Metropolitan College is currently developing a business case on the future of the Castlereagh campus. I have had no direct discussions with the College leadership or Governing Body in respect of this issue, however my officials continue to liaise closely with the College on the progress of the business case. The business case clearly setting out the justification, rationale and timelines to proceed with its proposed course of action will require my approval and the consultation responses will be assessed as part of that process.  I am aware of the strong community support expressed on the future of further education delivery in East Belfast.I remain fully committed to ensuring the Further Education estate is fit for purpose for the delivery of a high-quality curriculum and to ensure it will be financially sustainable into the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of whether the closure of Belfast Metropolitan's Castlereagh Campus is in alignment with her Department's strategic aims and vision for higher and further education in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: No decision has been made to close the Castlereagh Campus. Belfast Metropolitan College is currently developing the business case considering options for the future of the campus. The business case will require my approval and will need to clearly demonstrate continuing alignment with my Department’s key strategic aims and wider Economic Vision. I remain fully committed to ensuring the Further Education estate is fit for purpose for the delivery of a high-quality curriculum and to ensure it will be financially sustainable into the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the proposed closure of Belfast Metropolitan's Castlereagh Campus.
answer_text: I understand that Belfast Metropolitan College is developing a business case with options for the future of the Castlereagh campus, informed by the public consultation exercise. When complete, the business case will propose the recommended option and will be put to me for approval. No decision has been taken in respect of the campus at this stage. I remain fully committed to ensuring the Further Education estate is fit for purpose for the delivery of a high-quality curriculum and to ensure it will be financially sustainable into the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will review the criteria for nursery school places.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how the recent Supreme Court judgment in relation to biological sex will be implemented in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The matter is currently under consideration

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, given the recent Supreme Court judgment regarding biological sex, to detail any steps he will take to ensure single sex, female only changing rooms and toilets across local government and the community and voluntary sector.
answer_text: I welcome the judgement from the Supreme Court, which provides clarity in law. My Department is analysing the Supreme Court judgement and considering the implementation of the ruling on the policy responsibilities that fall to the Department. This process is not dependent on advice from the Equality Commission. I will be making a statement on this in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, given the recent Supreme Court judgment regarding biological sex, to detail any action she intends to take to ensure (i) prisons; and (ii) toilets and changing facilities across her Department are single sex spaces.
answer_text: The Equality Act 2010 does not apply in Northern Ireland but the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland has indicated that it will be working at pace to review guidance that it issues to public bodies about implementation of Section 75 duties in light of the Supreme Court judgment and make any amendments as necessary.My Department will consider any necessary changes to policies and procedures following full consideration of the Supreme Court judgment and will be informed by the updated guidance from the Equality Commission.  That includes any identified impact on the NI Prison Service and other buildings for which the Department is responsible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, given the recent Supreme Court judgment regarding biological sex, to detail any steps he will take to ensure single sex, female only toilets in public sector buildings.
answer_text: The Equality Commission is currently considering any potential implications here due to the Supreme Court ruling and will issue guidance in due course.This issue must be approached with compassion, understanding and dignity for all.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, given the recent Supreme Court judgment regarding biological sex, to detail any steps she will take to ensure single sex, female only changing rooms and toilets in the retail and hospitality sector.
answer_text: The Equality Commission is currently considering any potential implications here due to the British Supreme Court ruling and will issue guidance in due course. This issue must be approached with compassion, understanding and dignity for all.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline what protocols were agreed between his Department and the Department of Finance in relation to securing Dundonald House when it was vacated.
answer_text: Dundonald House was vacated by NICS staff on 5 June 2023. This followed an emergency health and safety issue that closed the building to all staff. Arrangements for immediate closure of the building were confirmed with my Department by the Department of Finance (DoF), as landlord for Dundonald House. At that point the building was closed and remained locked and secured. DoF arranged for security fencing to be erected around the perimeter of the building while it remained closed. When it was safe to do so, emergency access was granted to the building during normal business hours, however, this was under the supervision of security staff who provided access and locked the building again afterwards. Prior to the building reopening on 8 April 2024, for the purposes of decant, my Department sought clarity from DoF as to requirements necessary for ongoing protection of the building. On the basis of that engagement, and DoF’s assessment of the current risk profile for protecting the asset, from 8 April 2024 security cover was provided for the building during normal business opening hours. That has remained the case until the recent unauthorised access to the building in March 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any action his Department has taken to secure the Stormont Estate following breaches of security and incursions into Dundonald House.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Stormont Estate is a major tourist and visitor attraction which includes the Mo Mowlam Play Park and the NI Civil Service Sports Association facilities. The Estate is open to the public and entry is free. Opening times vary throughout the year but, in general, follow daylight hours with the current opening hours being Monday to Friday from 7.30 am to 8.00 pm and Saturday and Sunday from 9.00 am to 8.00 pm. Outside these hours, the perimeter entrances to the Estate are closed with 24-hour security available to provide essential entry. The Stormont Estate accommodates the Assembly and a number of Civil Service departments.  The Department of Finance (DoF) is responsible for security at the perimeter of the Estate. Security at buildings on the Stormont Estate is the responsibility of the largest occupying body in each building. The responsibility for security in Dundonald House passed to my Department on 28 March 2025. Following recent incursions, the pedestrian entrance adjacent to Dundonald House was closed and additional security patrols are in place, particularly around Dundonald House, but also on the wider Estate. There is now a 24-hour security presence in the building. Security measures across the Estate will be kept under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the date on which the Northern Ireland Prison Service vacated Dundonald House, particularly floors three and six; (ii) the date on which all documentation stored in Dundonald House was removed; (iii) the rationale for the decision to leave information in storage in Dundonald House when it was vacated; (iv) any risk assessment that was conducted prior to using a vacated building as storage for confidential documentation; (v) any information that was sought from the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs in relation to the security o
answer_text: (i) The Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) vacated Dundonald House on Friday 28 March 2025.(ii) All NIPS related personnel files were removed by Monday 24 March 2025.  All other files were removed by Wednesday 26 March.(iii) Dundonald House served as the Headquarters of the NIPS until June 2023, when the building was vacated with immediate effect due to Health and Safety concerns.  NIPS staff were temporarily decanted to Knockview Buildings and then Hillview Buildings, before moving to a new permanent HQ location at Castle Buildings in September 2024.  Given that both temporary locations and the new permanent HQ offer more limited space than that at Dundonald House, the need for a new file store was identified and raised with DoF and DoJ colleagues.  The preliminary date for completion of the file store in Castle Buildings was February 2025; however, changes and delays put the completion date back to September 2025. Dundonald House is located within the Stormont Estate, which is subject to controlled access and 24-hour security at the Massey Avenue entrance.  NIPS had a reasonable expectation that government buildings, particularly those on the Stormont Estate, are protected and secure from this type of activity.All NIPS files were held in secure cabinets within locked offices, with the keys held in secure key safes outside of Dundonald House.  These measures were deemed sufficient to mitigate any risk.(iv) The Prison Service carried out a risk assessment following the health and safety issue at Dundonald House in June 2023.  The aim of the risk Assessment was to consider the hazards that posed a risk to staff, identify controls already in place and what further actions could be taken to mitigate the risks related to those staff entering Dundonald House. (v) The focus of engagement with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) in the period following immediate evacuation of the building was on providing controlled access for staff to the files held securely within the building.  No concerns had been identified in respect of the security of the vacated building or the controls in place to ensure continued security of the files held within it, thus no information was sought from DAERA.(vi) No NIPS documentation, confidential or otherwise, remains within Dundonald House.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any discussions her Department has held with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs and the Department of Finance in relation to the securing of the building and the estate, further to the incursions into Dundonald House.
answer_text: Officials within the Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) engaged with their counterparts in the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs and the Department of Finance on a number of occasions following the incursions into Dundonald House.  Ultimately, in response to the incursions and following a review of risk, NIPS took the decision to put in place a range of additional physical security measures until the building could be fully vacated on Friday 28 March.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of security breaches and incursions into Dundonald House since it was vacated, including associated dates; (ii) the areas breached and by which Departments those areas were previously used; (iii) whether his Department was aware of the breaches prior to videos circulating on TikTok; and (iv) any action his Department took as a result of this knowledge.
answer_text: The Department of Justice (DoJ) and the Department of Finance (DoF) dealt with the recent intrusions to Dundonald House. I understand that DoJ reported two separate incidents to the PSNI of videos posted online on 16 and 17 March.  I also understand that there were further intrusions to the building on 18, 19 and 20 March and that DoF dealt with another intrusion to the building on 14 April, when security staff were on site and which it also reported to the PSNI. DoJ and DoF dealt with the recent intrusions to the building. DoJ has advised that the Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) occupied floors 2, 3 and part of floor 6 on the North side of Dundonald House, as well as four rooms in the basement.  It has also advised that, while intruders gained entry to floor 3, none of the locked rooms holding secure filing cabinets and files were breached. The intruders also accessed accommodation previously occupied by DAERA on floors 10, 6 North, 5 North and 1 North.  During the most recent intrusion to the building on 14 April, DoF has advised that intruders accessed the basement to the building. The basement area was previously used by several departments including DAERA and DoJ for storage purposes.The unauthorised access to Dundonald House occurred outside of normal opening hours for the building and my Department was not aware of the intrusion prior to the videos circulating on Tik Tok.Upon becoming aware of videos circulating on Tik Tok, officials from my Department immediately contacted officials from DoF and DoJ.  In response, DoJ officials advised that they were already aware of the unauthorised access and had reported the matter to the PSNI and DoF Stormont Estate Security. DoF officials subsequently advised that they were also aware of the matter and were dealing with it.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the current position in relation to security for Dundonald House, including how and when security has changed in the period since the building was vacated; (ii) the date on which Dundonald House was fully vacated; (iii) when other Departments that used Dundonald House, and still use Dundonald House for storage, were advised of the updated security arrangements; and (iv) any plans in place to fully secure Dundonald House to prevent further incursions.
answer_text: The Department of Finance (DoF) is currently responsible for security arrangements for Dundonald House. Security arrangements for the building previously operated on a full-time basis. However, this changed on 5 June 2023 when the building was closed to all staff as a result of an emergency health and safety issue.  When Dundonald House reopened to staff on 8 April 2024, for the purposes of decanting the building, security cover was provided during normal business opening hours. Following the recent unauthorised access to the building in March 2025, the Department of Justice (DoJ) arranged for prison personnel to be on site overnight and additional physical measures until it exited the building on 28 March. Since DoJ vacated Dundonald House, DoF has arranged for 24-hour security cover to be provided for the building.Dundonald House was fully vacated on 28 March 2025, following DoJ’s exit from the building.Departments that occupied Dundonald House were notified on 5 June 2023 that the building would immediately close to all staff. This included security staff as the building was deemed unsafe to occupy. On 8 April 2024, my Department confirmed arrangements with DoF, as landlord, for the ongoing protection of the building to facilitate its reopening at that time. Following the unauthorised access to the building in March 2025, DoJ advised DoF and my Department of additional security measures it had implemented at the building. DoF has confirmed that, since the unauthorised access to the building, a range of additional physical security measures have been implemented. DoF has also confirmed that 24-hour on-site security has been put in place within the building. The need for on-going or further security measures at or around Dundonald House will be kept under review by DoF, given its responsibility for the building.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail her Department's responsibility for roads being flooded from private land.
answer_text: I can confirm that the maintenance of drainage systems on private lands is the responsibility of the landowner. My Department is only responsible for the maintenance and cleaning of gullies and associated drainage systems within the adopted road network and any other lands within the Department’s control.  Under Article 53 of the Roads Order (NI) 1993, my Department may serve a notice on the owner or occupier of land adjoining a road, requiring them to do such works as are necessary to prevent, so far as is reasonably practicable, surface water from the land flowing onto or over the road.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) to detail the number of sites his Department is currently holding for potential special educational needs; and (ii) to list their locations, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: In my April 2024 statement on transforming capital investment to support children with special educational needs I highlighted that projections indicate a need for potentially up to eight entirely new special schools in the next 10 years.  The Education Authority (EA) has advised that pressures suggest four new schools are required in in the greater Belfast area and four across other areas in Northern Ireland.  My officials, alongside EA colleagues, are currently exploring a number of potential site options for these new schools, this includes existing sites already owned by the EA and third-party sites.I also announced that planning would commence immediately for new school builds for Sperrinview, Knockevin and a second campus at Ardnashee; site searches are also underway to identify potential sites for these schools. Identification of the preferred location for the entirely new special schools and the new builds will be subject to a feasibility report and the business case process.  I am therefore not in a position to confirm the location of any of the sites at this point.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he intends to review Housing Solutions.
answer_text: I have no plans to ask the Housing Executive to review its Housing Solutions service. Any review would be an operational matter for the Housing Executive. The Chief Executive has responded as follows:“Housing Solutions is the title used for the role fulfilled by Housing Advisors who deal with housing and homelessness applications. It should be noted that any proposed review will be carried out by Homelessness Policy, supported by the Business Review and Improvement Unit, within the Housing Executive and may consider aspects such as the job role, changes to the responsibilities of the job role since its introduction, and the priorities for the role moving forward.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly with regard to matters that are their responsibility. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update, including a time frame, on when the Housing Executive will be able to borrow finances to commence building social homes.
answer_text: I am continuing to actively push and prioritise a solution on NIHE Revitalisation, which will enable it to invest in its existing stock and contribute to new supply. Whilst I am unable to provide a timeline, I can assure you that I have taken every opportunity to raise this issue with the Executive and HM Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment made of the efficacy of the working arrangements and protocols between Housing Solutions and the Housing Executive.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive of the Housing Executive, who has advised me as follows.“Housing Solutions is the title used for staff dealing with housing and homelessness applications. This service is delivered via our local office network and by a bespoke unit in our Belfast Region. Allocations for social rented housing in Northern Ireland are governed by the Housing Selection Scheme which is a set of rules that applies to local social housing landlords. It is the responsibility of each respective landlord (i.e. Housing Executive or respective Housing Association) to ensure allocations are made in compliance with the scheme and therefore each respective landlord will have the final say on any allocations within their portfolio. All social housing providers have access to the Housing Management System which details information relevant to each individual application and information relevant to allocations will be shared via a Housing tab within this system. Additionally, staff within Housing Solutions will be available to address any queries for allocations officers on an ad-hoc basis as required. Information within the Housing tab will, where GDPR compliant, include evidence submitted by both the applicant, and/or gathered via data and information sharing protocols with other statutory agencies.” For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly with regard to matters that are their responsibility. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any progress made to strengthen the Housing Executive's ability to deal with problem tenants, including sanction, removal and refusal to rehouse.
answer_text: I am committed to ensuring that social landlords have appropriate powers to deal with unacceptable behaviour. A Consultation Outcome Report on proposals to amend existing anti-social behaviour legislation, specifically injunctions against anti-social behaviour and grounds for possession, was published in recent weeks. As part of the Fundamental Review of Social Housing Allocations, my Department is also working to bring forward legislation that will implement changes to eligibility for social housing and Full Duty homelessness status where there has been unacceptable behaviour. The proposals for a Housing Bill will be subject to the Executive’s legislative priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline what steps the General Register Office for Northern Ireland takes to notify other agencies and statutory bodies once a death has been registered.
To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the efficacy and accountability of Housing Solutions.
answer_text: In terms of the arrangements which the Housing Executive has in place to ensure the efficacy and accountability of its Housing Solutions service I have sought input from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows.“Housing Solutions is the title used for staff dealing with housing and homelessness applications. This service is delivered via the Housing Executive’s local office network and by a bespoke unit in the Belfast Region.Efficacy and oversight of the service is provided by Area offices and the Belfast unit who in turn report and account for the service to the Regional Manager. A monthly performance meeting, including Regional Managers, is held by the Director of Services at which all divisional activity, including Housing Solutions is reported upon. In addition, NIHE’s senior management team hold a monthly performance review meeting to consider performance of each Division. These arrangements seek to ensure appropriate efficacy and oversight and are complemented by regular reports to NIHE’s Board and via regular accountability meetings with the Department of Communities.”My Department has agreed and well-established oversight arrangements in place as provided for in the Partnership Agreement between the Housing Executive and DfC. The accountability meetings referenced above form one part of these oversight arrangements and are attended by the Housing Executive’s Chief Executive, its Directors and is chaired by the Deputy Secretary with responsibility for Housing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, following her statement on 11 March 2025 which referenced the potential need to introduce alternative delivery models for publicly-funded legal services, to detail how progressing this option will impact the current sequencing and timings of the Enabling Access to Justice Programme Delivery Plan.
answer_text: A consultation on the Enabling Access to Justice Programme Delivery Plan only formally closed on 27 March 2025 and the consultation on the associtated impact assessments is ongoing. The consulations are an important means of testing our proposed sequencing and ensuring we are examing issues in the right order and properly taking account of interconnectivities. I will not, therefore, be reaching any decisions on next steps, including those which may be impacted by work on alternative delivery models, until I have had the opportinuty to consider responses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the overall costs to cover internal staff movements while associate principals are in place in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: There are no associate principals in place in Northern Ireland and therefore there are no costs to cover internal staff movements while associate principals are in place.I understand that in  responses to questions to you in April 2024 it was explained that the Education Authority (EA) advised that it deploys persons with demonstrable experience as an effective school principal to undertake the responsibilities of principal in Controlled schools where the post of principal has become vacant and it is not possible to cover this vacancy on a full-time basis from within the staff who are employed at the school. In most instances, the persons engaged to undertake this role are recently retired school principals.These are temporary appointed principals and not associate principals.It was also explained in the responses in April 2024 that the EA had used this method to fill a principal position, on a temporary basis, on twenty occasions from September 2019 until April 2024. There has been one further engagement using this method since April 2024.

In most instances, the persons engaged to undertake this role are recently retired school principals.These are temporary appointed principals and not associate principals.It was also explained in the responses in April 2024 that the EA had used this method to fill a principal position, on a temporary basis, on twenty occasions from September 2019 until April 2024. There has been one further engagement using this method since April 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consideration she will give to instigating residents'-only parking schemes for small streets with a history of serious flooding where non-resident parking is causing difficulties for gully cleaning and other flood prevention measures.
answer_text: I am advised by my officials that if parked vehicles are causing an obstruction to the cyclical gully cleaning programme, these are generally noted by the contractor and a further attempt to clean will be made soon after.  If further attempts to clean the gullies are still unsuccessful then, in particular problem areas, plans may be made to enter the area at weekends or evenings, as resources permit. In the case of individual gullies which cannot be accessed, they may be left to the next cyclical clean unless there is a specific complaint.In relation to residents’ parking schemes, as outlined in AQW 24910/22-27, I have asked my officials to expedite the development of an application and assessment process for residents’ parking schemes to ensure that any requests can be appropriately considered. It is my intention to make an announcement as to how future schemes will be assessed by my Department over the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the recommendations and outcomes of the report on residents'-only parking schemes; (ii) the elements she plans to progress; and (iii) how residents can apply for their street to become eligible for such a scheme.
answer_text: My Department published The Residents’ Parking Review Report on 29 November 2024, which can be viewed at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/residents-parking-review-report.  Following the publication of the report there has been a significant level of interest for the establishment of residents’ parking zones across the north. Given the level of demand, and ongoing budgetary and resource constraints, I have asked my officials to expedite the development of an application and assessment process for schemes to ensure that any requests can be appropriately considered. This process will take into account the findings and recommendations of the report and provide a streamlined approach for considering, assessing and prioritising requests for residents parking schemes going forward. It is my intention to make an announcement as to how future schemes will be assessed by my Department over the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action she intends to take to reduce pavement parking.
answer_text: My Department sought the public’s views on options to tackle inconsiderate pavement parking and a Findings Report was published on 6 December 2023. Officials are currently developing the next steps for my consideration and a formal response will issue when I have determined the best way forward.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the current time frame for a feasibility study into flood alleviation options; (ii) how these are prioritised; and (iii) , given the flooding of homes in Park Avenue and Park Drive in Dundonald in November 2024, whether such a study will be prioritised in Dundonald, including when can it be expected.
answer_text: Dundonald has been identified within the NI Flood Risk Assessment as a ‘Transitional Area of Potential Significant Flood Risk’ and as a result is included on the Rivers Directorate Capital Works Programme. All potential schemes on the Capital Works Programme are prioritised based on a number of criteria including, any existing level of flood protection, benefit to cost ratio and estimated delivery cost. Due to its position relative to other schemes on this prioritised list, the feasibility study for this area has not yet commenced. However, work to update and review the computer model for the Enler River are on-going and due for completion in November 2025. These initial works will inform the assessment of flood risk within the feasibility study.It is currently anticipated that upon completion of the update to the computer model the feasibility study will commence in Spring 2026. This study will assess the flood risk to properties from the Enler river and identify if there are any economically viable schemes to help reduce the impact of river flooding. A feasibility study of this nature typically takes at least 18 months to complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a status update on CCTV cameras for the Dundonald Culvert which flooded in November 2024.
answer_text: My Department’s Rivers Directorate are currently establishing a contract for the supply of remote monitoring cameras. It is anticipated that the contract will be available within the coming months. This location will be a top priority for installing this digital infrastructure upon completion of the tender process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail who is responsible for clearing gullies on unadopted land, including car parks, if their blockage results in severe flooding, including the flooding of homes.

My Department is only responsible for the maintenance and cleaning of gullies and associated drainage systems within the adopted road network and any other lands within the Department’s control.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the services and facilities available in the Willows in Clarawood; (ii) the numbers and ages of children attending; (iii) how a child is referred to Willows; and (iv) how long they remain at this centre.
answer_text: (i)  Willow Education Otherwise than at School (EOTAS) is a short-term, part-time partnership programme of support for children with significant social behaviour and emotional wellbeing (SBEW) needs. An EOTAS placement provides a nurturing learning environment where children are enabled to develop their skills, become more aware of and responsive to others, develop confidence, and take pride in doing well and achieving success. Primary EOTAS centres support staff and pupils within their mainstream schools and also lead on the partnership arrangements for pupils on placement. Willow EOTAS centre staff provide in-school support through the service's telephone helpline, delivery of regional and whole school training, group-based intervention and supporting individual pupils. (ii) There are currently 9 pupils attending Willow EOTAS centre. Their ages range from 7 – 10 years old.(iii) Following meaningful engagement with the supports mentioned in response to point (i), the professionals involved may agree that a request be made, with the consent of the parent/ guardian, to the Primary EOTAS Panel for consideration of a part- time EOTAS Partnership Placement. (iv) Children attending EOTAS will successfully resume full-time attendance in their own class at their mainstream school as soon as it is felt they are able to do so. Pupils can remain on placement for up to three terms, depending on their presenting needs and progress on the EOTAS partnership programme in place.

answer_text: Housing Associations are responsible for assessing the condition of their own stock and developing appropriate asset management strategies to maintain and improve their properties. The Decent Homes Standard requires that Housing Associations “have a properly resourced maintenance and improvement programme to maximise the lettable life of their housing stock”. This is monitored at a high-level as part of Consumer Standard 2 of the Regulatory Framework for Registered Housing Associations.Guidance on the Decent Homes Standard, on the Fitness Standard and any related design guides or Building Regulations will all inform a Housing Association’s understanding of the shelf-life of components of housing and determine the content and timing of cyclical works programmes replacing and updating various elements (such as kitchens, bathrooms, heating systems, windows and doors).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the accountability processes for the Police Ombudsman's Office for Northern Ireland; and (ii) her Department's role in these processes.
answer_text: As stipulated in the Management Statement and Financial Memorandum, the Office’s performance, including the achievement of key objectives, shall be reported to and formally reviewed by the Department through the quarterly governance meetings. There is an annual accountability meeting with the Ombudsman and her Chief Executive each year to discuss the Office’s performance, its current and future activities, and any policy developments relevant to thoseactivities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) to detail the longest time it has taken for the Police Ombudsman's Office for Northern Ireland to investigate and report upon (a) a legacy case; and (b) a present day case; and (ii) to list those cases.
answer_text: The investigating and publishing of legacy and present day cases are operational matters for the Office of Police Ombudsman for Northern Ireland. You may wish to contact them directly to seek this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail how the Police Ombudsman's appeal against the recent High Court ruling will be funded.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for the Office of Police Ombudsman for Northern Ireland. Although the Office of the Police Ombudsman is funded from the Department of Justice budget, they are an independent body and how they allocate their budget is a matter for them to decide and report upon, within the parameters set out in their Management Statement Financial Memorandum (MSFM). question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) to detail the shortest time it has taken for the Police Ombudsman's Office for Northern Ireland to investigate and report upon (a) a legacy case; and (b) a present day case; and (ii) to list those cases.
answer_text: The investigating and publishing of legacy and present day reports are operational matters for the Office of Police Ombudsman for Northern Ireland. You may wish to contact them directly to seek this information.

answer_text: The investigating and publishing of legacy and present day cases are operational matters for the Office of Police Ombudsman for Northern Ireland. You may wish to contact them directly to seek this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of whether the strategic objectives and performance objectives within the Police Ombudsman's Office for Northern Ireland are being met.
answer_text: The Ombudsman’s Office have five strategic objectives, incorporating 50 targets of which 42 are on schedule for completion, or substantial progress has been made towards completion. The remaining 8 are not on schedule for completion this year. This is against a backdrop of the absence of the Ombudsman for a substantial part of the year; the appointment a new Chief Executive during the year after the post was vacant for some time, and the uncertainty around the impact of changes to the Northern Ireland Troubles (Legacy and Reconciliation) Act 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she intends to review the effectiveness of the (i) Police Ombudsman's Office for Northern Ireland; and (ii) Police Ombudsman.
answer_text: The performance of the Police Ombudsman’s Office is monitored through the governance processes set out in its Management Statement and Financial Memorandum.  There are no plans to review the effectiveness of the Office.Neither I nor my Department have the power to review the effectiveness of the Police Ombudsman. The Ombudsman is appointed under Royal Warrant, on the joint recommendation of the First and Deputy First Minister who have the legislative authority to consider the efficiency or effectiveness of the Police Ombudsman.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any performance appraisal process in place for the Police Ombudsman for Northern Ireland, including how often the process is conducted.
answer_text: The Police Ombudsman is independent and appointed as A Corporation Sole by the First and deputy First Minsters, acting jointly. Neither I, as Minister, nor my Department, have any role in a performance appraisal for the Police Ombudsman. There is an annual accountability meeting with the Ombudsman and her Chief Executive each year to discuss the Office’s performance, its current and future activities, and any policy developments relevant to those		activities. 						There is no provision in the Police Ombudsman legislation or Management Statement and Financial Memorandum (MSFM) for a performance appraisal for the Police Ombudsman.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the recent High Court ruling that the Police Ombudsman for Northern Ireland exceeded her legal powers.
answer_text: I have noted the judgement made by Justice Scoffield and will consider any implications it might have in due course. However, as the Police Ombudsman has lodged an appeal in regard to the judgement it would not be appropriate for me to make any comment at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice when the Police Ombudsman's tenure is due to finish.
answer_text: The Police Ombudsman’s appointment ends on 15 July 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update following the consultation on the proposed future location of the Knockbreda GP Out of Hours service.
answer_text: Belfast Health and Social Care Trust is responsible for the provision of GP Out of Hours services for the population of Belfast. The GP Out of Hours (GP OOHs) service is a core element of health and social care provision in primary care. Over recent years, there have been challenges in maintaining out of hours service provision at the two bases on the Crumlin Road and the Knockbreda Health and Wellbeing site.In October 2024 Belfast Trust launched a 13-week consultation, between 3 October 2024 and 2 January 2025, on the options for a one site GP Out of Hours Service on the Crumlin Road, with an enhanced Multi-Disciplinary Team to complement the GP workforce. Its aim was to give service users, families, staff and the public at large an opportunity to consider and comment on the proposed reorganisation of the service.The Trust has advised it received almost 900 responses to the consultation. In accordance with its statutory obligations, it will need time to consider these responses and the feedback from a series of engagement sessions with a range of stakeholders, including service users and staff to inform its decision making.  At this stage, no decision has been made but the Trust will publish an outcome report to ensure that all interested stakeholders are appraised on the Trust’s decision. A draft feedback report will be presented to the Executive Team and Trust Board in March for ratification before publication.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what consideration she has given to a distinct sickness policy for Prison Officers that is separate from the standard civil service policy.
answer_text: The HR Handbook policies apply to all civil servants, including Prison Service staff, and are regularly reviewed to ensure they reflect best practice and legislative changes. The Civil Service sickness absence policies are currently being reviewed. Any changes to these policies will be subject to consultation with Central Trade Union Side, including the Prison Officers’ Association which represents the interests of Prison Service staff. For Prison Service staff, the application of the sickness absence policies is carried out by a dedicated HR Strategic Business Partnering team which is embedded within the Prison Service. The staff in that team provide a bespoke service which is informed by a working experience of the prison environment and knowledge of the specific issues faced by Prison Service staff.  I therefore have no plans to create a separate sickness absence policy for Prison Service staff or to make amendments to the Civil Service HR Handbook policies that apply only to Prison Service staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a status update on the review of licensing laws.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what discussions his officials have had with their counterparts in the Department of Justice regarding improved information sharing with the Northern Ireland Prison Service relating to prisoners' health conditions and medications.
answer_text: Officials in my Department continue to engage with their counterparts in the Department of Justice regarding improved information sharing and some recent examples of improvement in this area are as follows:•	A Data Sharing Agreement between the NI Prison Service and the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust is nearing finalisation and is planned to be in place by January 2025.•	The new Health Encompass computer system is planned for rollout to Healthcare in Prison in 2025. A joint project is being set up to provide a two way interface between Encompass and the NIPS Prisoner Record and Inmate System Management to facilitate a digital solution to sharing agreed information.•	Further examples include the agreement and implementation in 2023 of a joint approach to supporting people at risk of suicide and/or serious self-harm. •	Also in 2023, NIPS and the Trust developed joint strategies on Suicide and Self-harm Risk Management; and Reducing the Harm Caused by Substance Use.•	The South Eastern Trust also supports NIPS staff by providing guidance on how best to communicate with people who have additional needs, for example those with ADHD and Autism Spectrum Disorder.•	The Trust does not share information on medication held in possession by individual prisoners. However NIPS and the Trust do have a joint Medicines Management forum under which a joint medicines management procedure is under development.In summary, NIPS and the Trust work hand in hand to meet the needs of people in custody. It is an example of how my Department and the Department of Justice working together is providing improved outcomes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any procedures that are still in place in (i) care homes; and (ii) nursing homes for Covid testing.
answer_text: The Care Home and Nursing Home sectors across Northern Ireland continue to experience fluctuating trends in the number of outbreaks of respiratory illness, however the outbreaks have become more limited in number, size and severity. This welcome trend is also seen in the much lower rates of mortality and morbidity associated with outbreaks. As we continue to live with COVID-19, the guidance on testing for COVID-19 in Care Homes/Nursing Homes has been reviewed. A letter was issued in February 2024 by the Public Health Agency to Care Home/Nursing Home and Hospice Managers. The letter sets out the updated guidance for COVID-19 testing in Care Homes/Nursing Homes and Hospices and provides new information on how to order LFD tests.The guidance letter covers the following areas: A.	Circumstances where testing is no longer required.B.	Use of testing for an individual symptomatic resident/patient for clinical management purposes - including eligibility for COVID-19 specialist drug treatments.C.	Outbreak management including Infection Prevention Control (IPC) measures and arrangements for reporting and monitoring. D.	Ordering new supplies of LFT tests.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his Department's review of the eligibility criteria for (i) free school meals; and (ii) uniform grants.
answer_text: My Department’s review of the eligibility criteria for free school meals and uniform grants has examined a range of options for increasing the numbers of children and young people who are able to access free school meals and uniform grants. My officials are currently finalising public consultation documents and associated impact assessments and I plan to launch a public consultation on the options considered by the review in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what discussions have been held with the administrators of the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme to address the lengthy timeframes for assessments and decisions.
answer_text: To date, £59 million has been paid to victims since the Scheme opened for applications on 31 August 2021. Departmental officials have regular and ongoing engagement with officials from the Victims’ Payments Board and the Department of Justice.  This includes monthly meetings to discuss any emerging issues, to keep the Scheme under review, and explore all opportunities to improve the process for assessments and determinations.Officials from TEO, the Victims’ Payments Board and DoJ, also meet regularly with the Scheme’s assessment provider, Capita, to look for ways to improve the timeframes for determinations.  The TEO Permanent Secretary met recently with the President of the Victims’ Payments Board to discuss progress on the Scheme and the current challenges.It is important to highlight that the complexities associated with the Scheme have a significant impact on application processing times. The historical nature of many of the incidents included in applications and the reliance on external partners for supporting information, are just two examples of the challenges that the Board is dealing with.We are also meeting shortly with the President of the Victims’ Payment Board to hear directly from him on the progress of the Scheme and the current challenges.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the mental health support that is in place for prison officers; and (ii) whether she will work with the Minister of Health to develop a mental health strategy for prison officers.
answer_text: Prison Officers have access to the NICS Welfare Support Services and the NICS Occupational Health Service.  They can also avail of Inspire Workplace Services and the NICS confidential Employee Assistance Programme (EAP) which provides bespoke counselling services. The NI Prison Service (NIPS) also provides additional support for Prison Officers through the Police Rehabilitation and Retraining Trust (PRRT) which provides a range of services including one to one counselling sessions.  NIPS’ partnership with PRRT continues to evolve and during 2023 over 400 operational staff received support through PRRT, including psychology and physio services; resilience training; and a Health and Wellness Programme developed specifically for prison staff.  Prison Officers can also seek support through the NIPS Critical Incident Response Stress Management (CISM) initiative, which provides a confidential and voluntary debriefing procedure to help normalise responses to minor, moderate and severe critical incidents.  In addition to this, in the previous mandate, I commissioned a review of support services for operational prison staff, which resulted in the extension of PRRT support services to retired officers.  That support is now operational and incorporates both psychology and physio support.  NICS HR within the Department of Finance is currently developing a Health and Wellbeing Strategy which will include mental health as a key component. As part of the out-workings of the Strategy, a Mental Health Forum (with representation from across the NICS and input from key external partners) will be established in October to further develop and promote staff services to support mental health and wellbeing.  These services will also be available to Prison Officers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, given that the nature of work in the Prison Service is different and distinct from other roles in the Northern Ireland Civil Service, and that the risk is higher, whether she will take steps to have post-traumatic stress disorder recognised in terms of sickness and ill health.
answer_text: All sickness absence within the Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) and wider Northern Ireland Civil Service (NICS) is categorised under a number of general categories for reporting purposes.  Post-traumatic stress disorder is recorded as an illness under the broad range of mental health illnesses, namely ‘Anxiety/Stress/Depression/Other Psychiatric illnesses’.  Occupational Health Service Medical Advisers are the Civil Service Pension Schemes medical advisers.  When making an assessment on ill health retirement they take into consideration the available medical evidence, including the content of specialist medical reports (where provided) for all reported conditions.  They apply their knowledge of the rules of the specific pension scheme that the individual is a member of in providing a balanced objective medical opinion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the mental health support that is in place for prison officers; and (ii) whether he will work with the Minister of Justice to develop a mental health strategy for prison officers.
answer_text: Mental health support in Northern Ireland is available to anyone who needs it including prison officers.  In recognition of the mental health needs facing our society, the Department’s Mental Health Strategy 2021 - 2031 sets out the future strategic direction of mental health services in Northern Ireland.  The Strategy was co-produced with a broad range of stakeholders including those with lived experience. The Strategy is person centred and takes a whole life approach.  It contains a number of actions which are designed to improve support, and provide the right support at the right time, to help people cope with mental illnesses and ensure individuals have equitable access to mental health services. I am aware that Prison Officers have access to the NICS Welfare Support Services and the NICS Occupational Health Service.  They can also avail of Inspire Workplace Services and the NICS confidential Employee Assistance Programme (EAP) which provides bespoke counselling services. The NI Prison Service (NIPS) also provides additional support for Prison Officers through the Police Rehabilitation and Retraining Trust (PRRT) which provides a range of services including one to one counselling sessions.  Mental Health is a cross departmental issue, and my Department is committed to supporting other Departments’ initiatives to put specific mental health supports in place.  As outlined in the Justice Minister’s response to AQW 15223/22-27, NICS HR within the Department of Finance is currently developing a Health and Wellbeing Strategy which will include mental health as a key component.  As part of the out-workings of the Strategy, a Mental Health Forum (with representation from across the NICS and input from key external partners) will be established in October to further develop and promote staff services to support mental health and wellbeing.  These services will also be available to Prison Officers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the Hyponatraemia Inquiry, for an update on the O’Hara recommendations, particularly on an individual duty of candour.
answer_text: Update on implementation of recommendations from the Hyponatraemia Inquiry 69 actions out of 120 actions arising from the 96 recommendations in the Inquiry into Hyponatraemia-Related Deaths (IHRD) report have been fully actioned. The remaining actions have been allocated to the appropriate policy area in the Department.  For example, there is a programme of work ongoing to address a further 25 actions through the ongoing Redesign of the Serious Adverse Incident Procedure and through development of a HSC Being Open Framework. Further, 11 actions relate to duty of candour and will be progressed further as part of that work (detail set out below). This accounts for 105 of the 120 actions. Of the remaining 15 actions, work is progressing across a number of these however, due to resource constraints, not at the pace my Department would like. Others require longer term policy projects which require dedicated resource. For example a number of actions would be addressed through a review of the RQIA’s founding legislation - the Health and Personal Social Services (Quality, Improvement and Regulation) (Northern Ireland) Order 2003. While my Department has identified this as a priority area, given resource constraints, the Department is required to make decisions in relation to the work that can be delivered within current resources. In that context, work on the review of the regulation is currently paused.Individual Duty of CandourI have said previously that I am in favour of every individual working in public service being honest and candid when people suffer unnecessary harm, whether physical, mental or psychological. That is my desired outcome. The question becomes how to create a regime that minimises the chill factor that deters individuals from being candid and maximises their willingness and responsibility to engage in an honest review of what went wrong. In addition to the related recommendations set out in the IHRD Inquiry, the Infected Blood Inquiry report, which was published in May 2024 and extends UK-wide, has made recommendations in relation to individual duties of candour – with consideration to extend these beyond healthcare. Further, the King’s Speech (July 2024) sets out a commitment to implement legislation introducing a duty of candour in the wake of the Hillsborough football stadium disaster. My officials are linked into discussions with counterparts in England, Scotland and Wales to consider these developments, which remain delicate and complex matters.
