The Framework aims to ensure that individuals are fully empowered to exercise candour and openness, and that HSC organisations have in place the necessary support and systems

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he intends to bring forward legislation in relation to an individual duty of candour.
answer_text: I am in favour of every individual working in public service being honest and candid when people suffer unnecessary harm, whether physical, mental or psychological. That is my desired outcome. The question becomes how to create a regime that minimises the chill factor that deters individuals from being candid and maximises their willingness and responsibility to engage in an honest review of what went wrong. In addition to the related recommendations set out in the IHRD Inquiry, the Infected Blood Inquiry report, which was published in May 2024 and extends UK-wide, has made recommendations in relation to individual duties of candour – with consideration to extend these beyond healthcare. Further, the King’s Speech (July 2024) sets out a commitment to implement legislation introducing a duty of candour in the wake of the Hillsborough football stadium disaster. My officials are linked into discussions with counterparts in England, Scotland and Wales to consider these developments, which remain delicate and complex matters. Options regarding next steps for Northern Ireland in relation to duty of candour will also take account of these discussions, and of related timeframes which remain under consideration.   My officials are also continuing work to develop the policy for a "Being Open Framework" for our health and social care system. The Framework aims to ensure that individuals are fully empowered to exercise candour and openness, and that HSC organisations have in place the necessary support and systems required to enable and to nurture a truly open culture.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given the difficulties its exclusion presents for criminal justice partners and those in the criminal justice system with the disorder, whether he will legislate to include personality disorder in the Mental Health (Northern Ireland) Order 1986.
answer_text: Currently under the Mental Health (Northern Ireland) Order 1986, a person suffering “by reason only of personality disorder” does not fall within the definition of mental disorder.  A person suffering from a personality disorder will only fall within the ambit of mental health legislation if diagnosed with a coexistent mental disorder. While there are currently no plans to reform the Mental Health Order, the Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 (MCA) will revoke the Mental Health Order for those aged 16 and over, when fully implemented.  The MCA is wide ranging and refers to “disorder” and includes any disorder or disability, whether mental or physical.  Specifically, “mental disorder” means any disorder or disability of the mind. The MCA applies to those who lack capacity in relation to a matter if, at the material time, the person is unable to make a decision for himself or herself about the matter because of an impairment of, or a disturbance in the functioning of, the mind or brain.  It does not matter whether the impairment or disturbance is permanent or temporary or what the cause of the impairment or disturbance is.Work continues to scope potential timeframes and costings for full implementation of the MCA, however a present there is no timeframe for this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, given the publication of Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency's statistics for sickness absence in the Northern Ireland Civil Service for 2023-24, which indicates that her Department had the highest levels of sickness absence across the Northern Ireland Civil Service, to detail (i) her assessment of the rationale behind such sickness absence levels; (ii) a breakdown of sickness absence levels, broken down by (a) section; and (b) arm's-length body; (iii) the cost to the public purse of the 2023-24 sickness absence across her Department; and (iv) how sh
answer_text: Reducing the level of sickness absence is a priority for my Department, as well as the Northern Ireland Civil Service. It is important, however, that there is an understanding and supportive approach in the management of sickness absence while also acknowledging the significant impact of ill-health and sickness absence on the cost and quality of service delivery.  All sickness absences within the Department are managed in line with the relevant NICS sickness absence policies. The aim of those policies is to promote regular attendance at work, minimise the level of absence, and to provide a framework within which sickness absence can be effectively managed.   It is also important to note that departmental staffing profiles can have a major influence on relative absence levels.  This needs to be considered when making Departmental comparisons, particularly given the high number of front-line operational roles within the DoJ, such as the Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS). My officials are engaging with colleagues in NICS HR within the Department of Finance to consider the most recent published statistics and potential management actions.  While the latest NISRA report includes an overall cost of sickness absence for the NICS, that information is not available for individual Departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why personality disorder is not included as a mental illness or disorder or disability of mind as per the Mental Health (Northern Ireland) Order 1986.
answer_text: Currently under the Mental Health (Northern Ireland) Order 1986, a person suffering “by reason only of personality disorder” does not fall within the definition of mental disorder. A person suffering from a personality disorder will only fall within the ambit of mental health legislation if diagnosed with a coexistent mental disorder. The definition of mental disorder is the main gateway for a number of provisions of the Mental Health (Norther Ireland) Order 1986, in particular, compulsory assessment and treatment. In Northern Ireland at present, a person suffering “by reason only of personality disorder” does not fall within this definition and cannot therefore be detained. This was because when the legislation was developed, it was felt that there was no treatment available and detention would not therefore be appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for a breakdown of costs and specification of the new kitchen and cafe in HMP Magilligan; (ii) to detail the extent of the increases in price; (iii) whether the specification has been reduced as a result in the increase of price; (iv) , on completion, how these facilities will compare to those in other prisons; and (v) whether there is an intention to increase prisoner numbers in HMP Magilligan as part of the long-term strategy for prisons and the redevelopment of the site.
answer_text: The current estimated cost of procurement and construction of a new kitchen and café at Magilligan Prison is £13.3m (including optimism bias and capital inflation). The Northern Ireland Prison Service are not in a position to provide a more detailed breakdown of costs or specification at this time.  The preferred option has not yet been approved, nor has the expenditure associated with the Outline Business Case been considered by either the Department of Justice or the Department of Finance.  Due to significant cost inflation, all capital cost projects, including this project, have been subject to price increases.  However, due to changes in design and scope of the project, costs have been reduced from initial estimates.  The specification of the project has not been affected by these changes. The new facility will provide a modern, fit for purpose kitchen and café to replace current ageing temporary structures that are of a poor standard.  Proposed facilities will be akin to those currently available at Maghaberry Prison and Hydebank Wood College and Women’s Prison.This project aims to support prisoners to gain catering and hospitality skills and qualifications that will support them to gain employment in the hospitality and service industries upon release, ultimately support a reduction in rates of reoffending and make Northern Ireland safer. Magilligan currently sits at maximum capacity of 500 prisoners due to the overall rise in prison numbers in Northern Ireland.  A business case is currently being developed for a new 60 cell accommodation block as part of the long term development strategy for Magilligan Prison.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the work of the Victim and Witness Steering Group.
answer_text: The Victim and Witness Steering Group continues to provide strategic leadership across criminal justice agencies and relevant Departments in order to improve the experiences of victims and witnesses of crime.  Membership includes senior leaders across the criminal justice system and as well as the Department of Health, victim representatives from the community and voluntary sector and representatives from the legal profession.The Steering Group oversees delivery against the Victim and Witness Strategy, and  The Victim and Witness Strategy Progress Report  sets out key achievements delivered under the current Strategy.At the last meeting, in June of this year, the Steering Group was updated on key targets within the current strategy. These include:Multi-agency work led by my Department, to develop and deliver a new, structured digital information portal for victims, “My Justice Journey”. An update on delivery of the “Victim and Witness Charters Communications Plan”, which has included promotion of the Charters at key community events, such as Belfast Pride and the Balmoral Show, as well as participation at a number of Police and Community Safety Partnership (PCSP) events.  An analysis of the impact of the recent multi-media public information campaign to raise awareness of the Charters. A survey of 1,000 respondents indicated that 84% felt the advertising was clear in communicating how someone should be treated as a victim or witness; and 86% felt it reassured people that a victim of, or a witness to, crime has rights that are protected.The Steering Group has also established a “Victim Data” Working Group to develop a framework for collating information on victims’ experiences across the Criminal Justice System and partner organisations, including compiling a dataset to measure compliance with the key entitlements of the Victim Charter.  The Data Group will report on its Phase 1 mapping exercise at the next Steering Group meeting on 5 November 2024. A current priority is the development of a new Victim and Witness Strategy. The Steering Group has endorsed plans for a Call for Views to inform the content of the new strategy. My Department plans to consult on the draft strategy early in the new year.Those members of the Steering Group who are senior leaders within justice organisations also perform the role of Victim Champions within their respective organisations.  Victim Champions have plans to co-host a Summit for criminal justice organisations on 24 October 2024 with the theme of ‘A Trauma Informed Approach to Supporting Victims’. Representatives from across the criminal justice system will explore mechanisms and toolkits to ensure a trauma informed approach is embedded within work practices, policies and procedures across all parts of the Criminal Justice System. The Steering Group will be responsible for ensuring that agreed actions from the summit are delivered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the fundamental review of criminal legal aid in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The report by His Honour Tom Burgess CBE on the fundamental review of Criminal Legal Aid in Northern Ireland was received on 23 August 2024. My officials are currently working through the report. Once this is complete and there is clarity on the resource and consequential impact of each of the recommendations, I will be engaging with Executive colleagues to bring forward an implementation plan and any necessary primary and secondary legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will implement a campaign in relation to pavement parking.
answer_text: The Department’s campaign about inconsiderate pavement parking has been running since November 2021. It demonstrates how inconsiderate parking can negatively impact on other road users, with the strapline ‘Think Before You Park’.  There are bus rear and radio adverts, and the campaign is routinely issued on the Department’s road safety website, Share The Road to Zero and its associated social media channels: @ShareTheRoadToZero on Facebook and @roadtozero for X, which carries the hashtag #ThinkBeforeYouPark, most recently between 16 & 22 August 2024.  The webpage  https://www.sharetheroadtozero.com/think-before-you-park provides further information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the Criminal Justice Improvement Group.
answer_text: The Criminal Justice Improvement Group (CJIG) was set up as a stakeholder group reporting to the Criminal Justice Board, looking at areas like cross justice modernisation and transformation. In January 2023, the Criminal Justice Board determined that its key priority would be to progress the Speeding Up Justice programme.  This has continued the drive to modernise and transform, and work has been continuing across the criminal justice system via a range of specific focused working groups.  Given the prioritisation of Speeding Up Justice by Criminal Justice Board and to avoid duplication of work at a time of limited resources, there have been no meetings of this group since September 2022.  However, ongoing proactive engagement has continued across Criminal Justice Organisations.

answer_text: The Prison Service Records Information System (PRISM) is used for the control and management of prisoners and records their latest address. question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of the benefit of a wider spectrum of voices in highlighting the financial needs of Northern Ireland for service provision.
answer_text: I am always keen to work collaboratively with groups and organisations to gain as much representation as possible.It is important that the Executive speaks with one voice and that approaches are led by the Department of Finance (DoF). It is a long standing convention and was also a recommendation of the RHI Inquiry, that DoF should be the sole conduit of formal communication with the Treasury.The financial pressures facing the Executive are well known and they are wider than just one department or body.I have raised the need for additional funding for public services with both the previous and the current British Government on numerous occasions including most recently when I met the Chancellor last week along with the First and deputy First Ministers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children of (i) primary school age; and (ii) post-primary school age that are currently on a waiting list for counselling services.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has confirmed, in terms of the Independent Counselling Service for Schools (ICSS), available through the EA to post-primary schools, there were 254 pupils in mainstream, EOTAS and special schools on the waiting list on 31 March 2024, across 259 schools.  There is currently no DE-funded counselling service available in primary schools. My Department does not hold information on waiting lists for counselling services across other government, voluntary and community sectors.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will build resilience training into the school curriculum.
answer_text: An overarching aim of the curriculum is to prepare each young person to develop as an individual, to achieve personal fulfilment and individual wellbeing through living a successful life.  Resilience is a key factor of good emotional wellbeing.  At all key stages throughout primary and post-primary, pupils are provided with opportunities to develop an understanding of how to maximise and sustain their mental health, self-esteem and wellbeing, including managing emotions and reactions to ongoing life experiences and enhancing self-awareness and developing coping strategies.  There are also a number of qualifications at key stage 4 and post-16 which provide opportunities for students to explore issues relating to self-esteem, mental health and wellbeing. The joint Department of Education / Department of Health “Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework” provides overarching guidelines to support those working in educational settings to help them promote emotional wellbeing and strengthen self-esteem and resilience in children and young people.  The Framework sets out examples of effective practice that includes a whole-school approach which helps develop the strengths and coping skills that underpin resilience.There are several services available to schools which may assist in the delivery of the curriculum and approaches to help build resilience in children and young people, including: CCEA’s Wellbeing Hub (providing curriculum linked resources and support for teachers); the Resilience in Education Assisting Change to Happen (REACH) programme for 6-19 year olds led by the Education Authority’s (EA) Youth Service; and the Being Well Doing Well programme led by the EA (supporting schools to develop a whole-school approach to emotional health and wellbeing).  In addition, I recognise the importance of schools having flexibility around the type of support provided to pupils and schools may wish to engage with external services including the voluntary and community sector for other supports, programmes and services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what Equality Impact Assessment or Human Rights Assessment have been conducted on people living in temporary accommodation.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has statutory responsibility for responding to homelessness, including provision of homelessness accommodation. As such I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive is subject to Section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act and the Human Rights Act in relation all its policies and functions. This includes our comprehensive approach to Homelessness and households placed into temporary accommodation. The Housing Executive’s strategic approach to addressing homelessness is outlined in the Homelessness Strategy 2022-27 available via:https://www.nihe.gov.uk/getattachment/73313718-aa0e-4aae-b122-6573dcab88c7/Ending-Homelessness-Together-Homelessness-Strategy-2022-27.pdf…which was subject to an equality screening process which can be found on: https://www.nihe.gov.uk/getattachment/c674990a-3b42-450d-a7c3-337bdea94ccf/Homelessness-Strategy-Equality-Screening.pdf”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of children currently living in temporary accommodation.
answer_text: The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin July - December 2023 contains information on the numbers classified as experiencing homelessness and is available here:https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-july-december-2023 As of 11 January 2024, there were 4,844 children living in Temporary Accommodation.The publication of the next bulletin is scheduled for June 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the treatment of many children placed in temporary accommodation.
answer_text: As this is a matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, I have asked for an assessment from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive will always seek to provide temporary accommodation that meets the needs of the individual households, and this will be particularly relevant when households include children. The vast majority of placements in temporary accommodation for households with children are in voluntary sector hostels or Single Lets, and for those households placed in non-standard accommodation, there will always be an emphasis on assisting the household as quickly as possible to alternative temporary accommodation, or in some cases permanent housing.It should be noted that while the Housing Executive will endeavour to provide a placement that as far as possible, meets the needs of the respective household, since the onset of the pandemic there has been a sustained increase in demand for temporary accommodation. In such circumstances, any provision of temporary accommodation will be dictated by availability on a given day.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of children living in temporary accommodation who have been offered housing more than 35 miles from their current place of residence.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive does not routinely record details on the number of children living in temporary accommodation by distance from their last settled address. The most recently published data, as per the DfC Homelessness Bulletin, noted a total of 4,844 children in temporary accommodation as of January 2024. While the Housing Executive is unable to provide this information it should be noted that the Housing Executive will always strive to place households in temporary accommodation that meets the needs of the respective household, and particularly when children are in the household.” For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people currently living in temporary accommodation in Belfast.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.As such I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive of the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, who has advised me as follows:“As of 31st December 2023 there was a total of 2,250 households within the Belfast City Council area and who were placed in temporary accommodation. It should be noted that the 2,250 households refers to households who presented as homeless within the Belfast City Council area.” For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with the Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) how much of the £75.3 million, allocated to her Department following the restoration of devolution, was received by the Northern Ireland Prison Service; (ii) for what purposes; and (iii) how much of such allocation went towards the 2023-24 pay award.
answer_text: £75.3 million was allocated to my Department as a total Resource DEL allocation to cover the Department’s forecast overspend, including pay pressures.The Northern Ireland Prison Service received £5.017m additional funding in year, which was used to meet pressures arising from pay awards for 2023/24.  This enabled the Prison Service to implement all of the Prison Service Pay Review Body recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the Working Group on Remand.
answer_text: The Working Group on Remand, chaired by the Director General of the Northern Ireland Prison Service, was established in November 2022 to explore viable alternatives to remand and, more recently, to provide oversight of the delivery of recommendations contained within the Criminal Justice Inspection Report on the ‘Operation of Bail and Remand in Northern Ireland’.   I am keen to ensure the Judiciary has a range of viable alternatives to remand available to them.  Therefore, I welcome the work the Group are progressing, which includes: developing a prospective bail support pilot for women through a (soon to be established) multi-agency group; enhancing the availability and use of management information on bail and remand; and exploring the range of electronic monitoring technologies available to support individuals in the community whilst maintaining public safety.  I am acutely aware that many of the initiatives will require significant incremental investment which, as you will appreciate, could prove challenging.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the work of the Criminal Justice Board to detail (i) its workstreams; (ii) the lead agency for each workstream; and (ii) progress reports thus far.
answer_text: The Criminal Justice Board (CJB) is the main strategic oversight group for the criminal justice system in Northern Ireland.  The Board is responsible for overseeing and progressing justice-led Programme for Government commitments, agreeing shared priorities across justice, monitoring progress towards achieving these and addressing key strategic issues affecting the criminal justice system.  The Board promotes opportunities for co-ordination and joint working to improve: effectiveness; public confidence in the criminal justice system; and the experience of victims, witnesses and defendants.Whilst the development of criminal justice policy is the responsibility of the Minister of Justice, the Board plays an important role in delivering cross-cutting initiatives. The independence of Board members and their respective organisations is recognised and respected. The key focus of the Board over the last 16 months has been work to reduce avoidable delay in the Criminal Justice System, ‘Speeding Up Justice’. The agreed ‘Speeding Up Justice’ workstreams to progress this priority along with the lead agency are:Committal Reform (CJB Sponsor - Director of Access to Justice)Early Engagement (CJB Sponsor - Director of Public Prosecutions)Out of Court Disposals (CJB Sponsor  - Chief Constable)The Remit of the Magistrates’ Court (CJB Sponsors – Director Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service)Digital (CJB Sponsor - Director of Access to Justice)Progress has been made across each of the workstreams, however, the pace and scope of change will be greatly dependent on funding.  A brief update on each workstream is provided below.Committal Reform (CJB Sponsor - Director of Access to Justice)The first phase of Committal Reform was introduced on 17 October 2022, when the option of calling victims and witnesses to provide oral evidence during committal proceedings in the magistrates’ court was abolished.  Since implementation, the Department has been monitoring progress and no issues have been reported to date.  So far over 1,000 cases have been committed to the Crown Court under the new provisions.Legislation for Phase 2 of committal reform has been made but not commenced; this will see certain cases directly committed to the Crown Court.  This is a complex change to a longstanding process, and detailed work is currently underway including preparing the necessary changes to court rules, the development of new Legal Aid fees and changes to case management systems across the criminal justice system.  Implementation of Direct Committal will need to take into consideration a number of factors including whether the courts are sufficiently recovered to cope with this significant change in practice.Early engagement (Sponsored by Director of Public Prosecutions, PPS)The Early Engagement workstream explores opportunities for improving how key stakeholders such as police, prosecutors and defence representatives communicate and engage during case progression.  Progress has b

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will review the criteria for nursery places to better assist working parents.
answer_text: The admission criteria for pre-school settings are set by the settings themselves, although legislation requires that settings give priority to children from “socially disadvantaged circumstances”. I am aware that this legislation needs to be reviewed. A consultation on the matter ran from 19 April to 16 July 2021 and confirmed that there is no longer support for the indefinite retention of this statutory priority criterion.   It is my intention to move to a position where 22.5 hours is the universal offer and the socially disadvantaged criterion can be removed completely.  This will take some time to achieve and will be subject to agreement and significant investment by the Assembly.In the meantime, I can reassure you that there are sufficient places in every area across Northern Ireland to provide a funded pre-school education place for every target aged child whose parents want it. The pre-school admissions process for September 2024 is currently underway. By the end of stage one of the process, 98.33% of children had already been offered a funded pre-school education place, with 89.2% of applicants being offered a place at their parents’ first preference setting.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the Prison Population Oversight Group.
answer_text: The Prison Population Oversight Group was established in November 2023.  Through this Group, senior leaders from across the justice system and partners including the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust and the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, have examined the contributing factors and the opportunities available to limit or mitigate the impact of the rising sentenced and remand prison population. The Group is scheduled to meet later this month to consider opportunities that could be delivered within the next two years, and within confirmed budget allocations.  It is likely that implementation and oversight will be provided through existing justice governance arrangements, and with appropriate engagement with the Criminal Justice Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline her action plan to reduce the amount of unpaid rates.
answer_text: Over £1.50 billion has been collected in this rating year. This is the largest amount collected by LPS within a rating year and £140M more than last year. Rating debt has increased since 2020, contributing factors include the impacts from the Covid Pandemic and the cost of living/doing business crisis.Land & Property Services (LPS) has robust billing, collection and recovery processes in place to maximise rate collection and reduce rating debt.  These processes include issuing annual rate bills and final demands for payment.  Where payment or arrangements to pay are not made, LPS will initiate recovery procedures. This includes formal written correspondence, e-mails and direct telephone contact with ratepayers.LPS will progress legal recovery actions for persistent non-payment.  This includes progression through the Magistrate Courts, the Enforcement of Judgement’s Office and initiation of bankruptcy proceeding. Ratepayers experiencing financial difficulties are encouraged to contact LPS to discuss payment options which can include restructuring current payment plans or establishing longer term payment arrangements to enable payment of rate arrears.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will retain the inclusion of death, dying and bereavement in a new Programme for Government, as agreed in the 2017-2022 mandate.
answer_text: As an Executive we are fully committed to treating everyone – at every stage of their lives – with dignity and respect. People deserve to have access to the services they need, throughout and at the end of life. Work is now moving at pace to develop a new draft Programme for Government with the aim of producing a final agreed Programme for Government by summer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what discussions she has had with the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs regarding an animal cruelty register.
answer_text: I have not had any formal discussions with the current Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) regarding an animal cruelty register, as policy responsibility for the welfare of animals sits with DAERA. Responsibility for measures to combat animal cruelty offences is, therefore, a matter for that Department. However, I am open to discussing with the DAERA Minister how best my Department can support his with respect to the penalties and fines attached to animal welfare offences, whether this is through a register or otherwise.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will bring forward proposals on an animal cruelty register.
answer_text: I am aware of the case for an Animal Cruelty Register as part of combined efforts to tackle Animal Cruelty in Northern Ireland. Whilst this has been explored previously by my Department and operational challenges have been identified to enable roll out, if Assembly colleagues or stakeholders can provide additional evidence to support the establishment and operation of a register, which includes where any registers have worked effectively elsewhere, my officials will consider any details received. I am conscious that an objective of any such register would be to ensure, as much as possible, that those banned from keeping animals do not obtain animals again. In terms of Animal Welfare legislation more generally, I am reviewing matters with officials and, once complete, intend to engage with stakeholders as we look towards delivering stronger protections against Animal Cruelty.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people on the housing waiting list in East Belfast listed as in Housing Stress.
answer_text: The number of applicants on the social housing waiting list in East Belfast listed as in housing stress is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.You should, therefore, direct your question to the Housing Executive at its dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he intends to review the Housing Executive’s points allocation system.
answer_text: A public consultation on a review of the Housing Selection Scheme was launched in 2017 and an outcome report published in 2020. Proposals are now being taken forward, with the first changes  implemented on 31 January 2023. Work is ongoing to implement proposals within the second phase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will review the policies, sanctions processes and thresholds to deal with persistent Housing Executive problem tenants.
answer_text: My Department is committed to ensuring that social landlords have appropriate policies and necessary statutory powers in place to deal with problem tenants. The Housing Executive is required to publish its policies and procedures in relation to anti-social behaviour and has a range of powers at its disposal for dealing with these issues. My Department keeps these powers under review to ensure that they are working effectively.To this end, as part of a joint public consultation with the Department of Justice, my Department recently published proposals to amend existing anti-social behaviour legislation including grounds for possession and injunctions against anti-social behaviour. I will consider the recommendations and next steps in due course.

answer_text: The Healthy Happy Minds Pilot concluded on 31 March 2023. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the impact of delays in the planning system on investment in Belfast.
answer_text: An efficient planning system is key to maximising investment.  Improving our planning system will require collaborative working by all parties involved.  My Department will play its part in delivering the planning system we need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) whether she will introduce legislation similar to Charlotte's Law; (ii) what the timeframe will be for the introduction of legislation; and (iii) when she will publish the findings of the public consultation on Charlotte's Law from November 2021.
answer_text: It remains my intention to bring ‘Charlotte’s Law’ forward during the current mandate as part of a Sentencing Bill.Officials in the Department have continued to develop the detail of the proposal working closely with the affected families and other stakeholders  during the absence of the Assembly.  The findings of the public consultation along with a Way Forward report will be published once this development work is complete.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission (i) what investigations it has conducted into the cause of flies in rooms at the rear of Parliament Buildings, and (ii) what action it is taking to address the issue.
answer_text: Building Services were made aware that some offices on the third and fourth floors were experiencing large numbers of flies during the month of October.  As such a pest control company were tasked with investigating the issue and treating accordingly.It was determined by the pest control operative that there was no underlying reason for the flies outside of the possibility that it was due to the unseasonably mild weather.  Building Services will continue to monitor these offices and carry out further investigations if required.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether it will publish a Modern Slavery Statement.
answer_text: Commercial organisations, as defined by the Modern Slavery Act 2015, are required to publish a Modern Slavery Statement.  Therefore, the Northern Ireland Assembly Commission has not published a Modern Slavery Statement.  However, the Assembly Commission’s procurement process requires potential suppliers to declare whether they are a relevant commercial organisation as defined under the Modern Slavery Act 2015 and, if so, to demonstrate compliance with the associated annual reporting requirement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action he is taking to (i) raise awareness of Sarcoidosis; and (ii) increase training for medical professionals to identify symptoms early.
answer_text: Sarcoidosis is a rare disease. I published the Northern Ireland Rare Diseases Action Plan on 22 March 2022. The Action Plan sets out 14 high-level actions to improve the lives of people living with a rare disease in Northern Ireland, including actions around raising awareness of rare diseases and training among healthcare professionals. The Action Plan is being taken forward by my Department, working together with healthcare professionals and educators, researchers and commissioners, as well as the Northern Ireland Rare Disease Partnership (NIRDP) on behalf of those personally affected by rare disease.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will (i) revisit the decision to permanently remove all existing wood pole mounted lights at the end of their life span; and (ii) consider replacing the wood piles instead.
answer_text: My Department has many street lights which are mounted on wooden poles, in particular in rural areas. These wooden poles and the associated overhead cables are the property of Northern Ireland Electricity (NIE).When these poles and cables reach the end of their lives and are removed by NIE, my officials will carry out an assessment of the area to see if it meets the current policy for the provision of street lighting. Replacement lighting will generally be installed in those areas which meet this policy, however if the area does not fulfil this criteria, the lighting will not be replaced.This policy helps ensure replacement lighting is provided where it is most needed as well as reducing future maintenance and energy costs and environmental impacts.V

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many women have been referred to hospitals in Great Britain or the Republic of Ireland to receive blood patch treatment for Cerebrospinal Fluid leakage.
answer_text: Information on referrals to Great Britain or the Republic of Ireland for treatments of cerebrospinal fluid leakage is not held within my Department. Each of the five Health and Social Care Trusts were approached for this information. South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust provided a response from a consultant anaesthetist working at the Ulster Hospital, since 2001. They stated that they are not aware of any woman from their maternity unit being referred to any other unit, within Northern Ireland or further afield, for the management of an acute cerebrospinal fluid leak following an epidural or spinal procedure on their labour ward.All of the other Health and Social Care Trusts were unable to provide a response in the timeframe provided as data are not held centrally and could only be provided at disproportionate cost.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will review the policy for accessible parking bays in residential areas to make sure (i) the applicant has priority use and; (ii) that having one accessible bay in the area does not preclude other applications in close proximity.
answer_text: While a Blue Badge holder has applied for the provision of an on-street accessible parking bay, this parking space can be used by any vehicle displaying a valid Blue Badge. The presence of an accessible parking bay does not necessarily preclude the approval of other applications however the policy says that ‘consideration should be given to the number of existing bays in a street before providing any additional ones. Accessible/Blue Badge bays should generally not account for more than 10% of the available on-street parking in any particular street’. This seeks to ensure a balance in the parking space provision. While I do think a review of the policy and its application has merits there is simply not enough time in this mandate to conduct such a review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the process when an applicant applies for, or renews, a driving licence and ticks the box to state that there has been a history of alcohol dependence.
answer_text: The Road Traffic (Northern Ireland) Order 1981 (the Order) sets out the requirements on the Department to be satisfied as to the medical fitness of an applicant for a driving licence in relation to relevant or prospective disabilities. Where a driver declares that they have a medical condition, either on first application for a driving licence, applying for a renewal licence, or during the lifetime of an existing licence, the Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) is required to be satisfied that the driver meets the required medical fitness to drive standards as part of their licensing consideration.  On receipt of information that the driver may be suffering from alcohol misuse or dependency, the driver will be issued the relevant medical form for their General Practitioner (GP) to complete and return to the DVA.On receipt of the completed information from the GP, DVA staff will assess the information provided and will consider whether the case should be referred to the qualified medical officers in the Occupational Health Service (OHS) who carry out the medical assessments on fitness to drive on behalf of the DVA.  The medical officers in OHS may be able to assess the case on the information provided, request further information or refer the case for specialist medical assessment.The standards in relation to medical fitness to drive are set out in the Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency’s guide Assessing fitness to drive - a guide for medical professionals.  While the guidance is published by the DVLA it is also applicable here.  The relevant section in relation to alcohol misuse or dependency is set out at Chapter 5 of the Guide.  Each case is considered on its own merits and assessed against the standards set out in the guide.  The medical officers will determine what medical investigations are required in order to make a recommendation to the DVA in relation to the applicant’s medical fitness to drive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) under what circumstances carbohydrate-deficient transferrin blood testing is used in applications or renewals to DVLA; and (ii) how many times it has been used over the last three years.
answer_text: The Driver & Vehicle Licensing Agency (DVLA) in Great Britain (GB) is responsible for the administration of driver licensing for those drivers who are resident in GB, the Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) is responsible for the administration of driver licensing for drivers who are resident here.  Carbohydrate deficient transferrin (CDT) blood testing may be used when assessing medical fitness to drive for drivers who are being medically investigated where there is concern regarding alcohol abuse or dependency.  A CDT test may also be carried out where drug or substance misuse is being investigated and alcohol abuse or dependency is clinically indicated.    In addition, CDT is also used in the relicensing consideration of drivers who fall within the High Risk Offender (HRO) scheme.   HRO drivers are those who meet criteria set out Regulation 52 of the Motor Vehicles (Driving Licences) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1996, as follows:  Following disqualification by order of a court for driving or being in charge of a vehicle when the level of alcohol in the body equalled or exceeded set levels,  where the driver failed to provide a specimen when required to do so, where the driver has been disqualified by order of a court on two or more occasions within a period of 10 years for driving or being in charge of a vehicle with excess alcohol, or while unfit through alcohol.In relation to the number of times CDT testing has been used over the last three years, while the DVA holds the information requested, it is not held in a form which is easily extracted.  Around 20,500 cases were referred to the medical advisors in the Occupational Health Service (OHS) for medical assessment in relation to driver licensing in the 2021/22 financial year.  In order to assess which cases were referred for assessment of alcohol abuse or dependency, the DVA would be required to examine each record and then establish whether a CDT test had been conducted.  This information could only be obtained at a disproportionate cost.I can confirm that since December 2021, when CDT testing became part of the process in the relicensing consideration of drivers who fall within the HRO scheme, 672 drivers have had a CDT assessment completed under the HRO scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the status of the Fresh Start Through Sport programme.
answer_text: The Department will provide funding of over £191,000 this year for the Fresh Start through Sport Programme. So far in 2022/23 the programme, which is delivered in partnership with the Irish Football Association, Ulster GAA, Ulster Rugby and the Belfast Giants, has been delivered in East Belfast.  The programme will also be delivered as planned in the remaining target areas of Ards, Lurgan and Derry and Strabane between now and 31 March 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how often Employment and Support Allowance carries out reviews of cases to prevent (i) fraud applications; and (ii) overpayments.
answer_text: The Employment and Support Allowance Centre has a range of review mechanisms in place to prevent fraud.  At the outset of a claim, evidence is sought in all cases to support information provided as to an individual’s circumstances, to ensure awards subsequently made reflect entitlement. During the lifetime of a claim, Occupational Pension and Capital reviews are conducted on an annual basis with customers to prevent irregularity of payment arising from a change in circumstances.In addition, the Department’s Benefit Security Division carries out counter fraud activities on Employment and Support Allowance cases which ranges from case reviews to criminal investigations for the most serious frauds.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether Recommendation B5 of the Fresh Start Programme is the only initiative not currently operating.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how much money from funding for mental health has been allocated to frontline services and charities, that provide services to people in mental health crisis, in the last five years, broken down by organisation.
answer_text: The information requested is not held in a consistent, reportable format across each of the HSC Trusts, PHA and Strategic Planning and Performance Group (formally the Health and Social Care Board).  Retrieval would require a significant manual exercise which would be at a disproportionate cost.However, my Department provides £507k of core funding per annum for 11 regional Mental Health voluntary organisations.  This funding has been in place over the period of the last five years, with the exception of 2020/2021 where I made the decision to increase the core grants by 25% for one year.  This resulted in £632k being made available for the 11 regional Mental Health voluntary organisations in that year.  Given the range of services offered by the funded voluntary and community mental health organisations, it is not possible to determine how much funding is provided for services to people in mental health crisis.I also launched a new Mental Health Support Fund in April 2021 to equip and enable our local charity sector to continue to provide vital mental health services in our communities.  A total of £16.02m has been made available for the Fund and this has allowed in excess of 100 projects to be supported across a range of charities and organisations offering mental health services across Northern Ireland.  Whilst it is not possible to highlight which are specifically directed at crisis-specific interventions, details of successful organisations along with the level of funding awarded, can be accessed via the following links: Mental Health Support Fund Round 1 Successful ApplicantsMental Health Support Fund Additional Funding and Successful Applicants

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) how Sport NI sets its criteria for funding programmes; (ii) how projects are scored or awarded against the criteria; and (iii) for her assessment of the method Sport NI uses to score each application and how consistently this method is applied.
answer_text: Criteria are established to meet the objectives of each individual funding programme; guidance notes to assist applicants are published in advance. Applications are then scored against programme criteria, and awards made on that basis. As an organisation, Sport NI is committed to continuous improvement and as part of their annual assurance processes, have identified a number of actions to further enhance the system of internal control for grant administration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of how Sport NI sets out its funding criteria and the transparency of its funding processes.
answer_text: Sport NI’s funding criteria and supporting guidance notes are published on its website for applicants to reference.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the organisations that have received funding from Sport NI; (ii) the programme under which the funding was allocated; and (iii) the amount and purpose of the funding allocated, broken down by constituency, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Details of funding paid by Sport NI in each of the last five years is recorded on the Government Funded Database and publicly available at https://govfundingpublic.nics.gov.uk/Home.aspx

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what methods are used to detect Cerebrospinal Fluid leaks in (i) Northern Ireland; (ii) other regions of the UK; and (iii) the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, a variety of approaches can be used to detect suspected cerebrospinal fluid leaks depending on a clinical assessment of a range of factors including the patient’s history, the timing of onset and nature of symptoms, possible causes of symptoms where known (e.g. if linked to a clinical procedure or other causes such as trauma), examination findings from a potential range of imaging techniques, and any relevant tests.  Northern Ireland practice is aligned to UK training and clinical standards so will be comparable to other UK regions.  The Department does not hold information on the methods used to detect cerebrospinal fluid leaks in the Republic of Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how the success rates of detection of Cerebrospinal Fluid leaks using imaging in Northern Ireland compares with (i) other regions in the UK; and (ii) the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: As each case of cerebrospinal fluid leakage is unique, with multifactorial causes and a range of available diagnostic methods based on the patient’s individual circumstances and symptoms, there is currently no known reliable or comparable data available that would enable a quick response to this question.  A literature review would be required to provide a reliable comparison and a comprehensive answer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what advice is given to patients prior to receipt of an epidural; and what measures have been taken to ensure it is given consistently across Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: Prior to administering an epidural to a patient, the clinical team will seek the appropriate patient consent for the procedure.  As part of the consent process for an epidural procedure, patients are typically provided with an epidural information card. This card generally includes an explanation on the setting up of the epidural, how long it usually takes to work, potential advantages of the procedure, possible problems and potential risks. The anaesthetist performing the epidural will also discuss the procedure with the patient and answer any questions a patient may have.In ensuring that this type of information and procedures are applied consistently across the health care sector, my Department, on 23 May 2022, endorsed ‘NICE Clinical Guideline NG197’, which covers how to make shared decision-making part of everyday care in all healthcare settings. This guideline includes recommendations on how to communicate the risks, benefits and consequences of a procedure, using decision aids, in a shared decision-making process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what information is given to GPs to alert them to Cerebrospinal Fluid leakage and symptoms.
answer_text: Currently there is no specific information given by SPPG/DOH to GPs on Cerebrospinal Fluid leakage and symptoms.  However, GPs are expected to identify and undertake educational activity based on their own assessment of their professional development needs and this is facilitated through compulsory annual appraisal. GPs are also expected to be familiar with relevant local and national guidelines and refer to these as required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many women are paying for private treatment in Great Britain or the Republic of Ireland hospitals to receive (i) blood patch treatment; or (ii) any other treatment for Cerebrospinal Fluid leakage.
answer_text: Information on women paying for private treatment in Great Britain or the Republic of Ireland for blood patch treatment or other cerebrospinal fluid leakage treatment is not held centrally within my Department. Each of the five Health and Social Care Trusts in Northern Ireland were asked if they hold this information with each replying that such information is unavailable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what is the best treatment for Cerebrospinal Fluid leakage, six to eight weeks after childbirth/diagnosis.
answer_text: Treatment for cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) leakage will be determined on the basis of clinical decisions in accordance with an individual patient’s clinical needs.  The services generally available for patients in Northern Ireland with CSF leaks will depend on the cause.  When there has been a procedure related cause, such as those directly related to epidural/spinal anaesthesia during childbirth, or where a diagnostic/therapeutic lumbar puncture has been performed, these will be managed conservatively in the first instance.  In the event that this is unsuccessful, all Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts provide a blood patch service, where this is clinically appropriate.For other types of CSF leak, for example relating to complex spinal or cranial surgery, cases are managed on an individual basis and where clinically appropriate will also be referred outside Northern Ireland in order to receive the appropriate advice and care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what funding is available for patients to receive blood patch treatment in Great Britain, following a diagnosis of Cerebrospinal Fluid leakage; and (ii) how this funding is accessed.
answer_text: Whilst there is no specific funding available for patients to receive blood patch treatment for a CSF leak in Great Britain, patients are considered on an individual basis and can be referred for treatment where there is a clinical need that cannot be assessed or treated currently within the HSC. Should a patient’s consultant consider it necessary and the specific procedure is not available locally in Northern Ireland, my Department will consider funding an extra-contractual referral (ECR) to GB, if the local consultant demonstrates clinically exceptional grounds.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many women have received blood patch treatment for Cerebrospinal Fluid leaks in each of the last five years, broken down by hospital; (ii) how many had to receive one or more blood patch treatments; (iii) what was the frequency of the treatments; and (iv) what was the time period between treatments.
answer_text: Information on treatments of cerebrospinal fluid leaks is not held within my Department and so each of the five Health and Social Care Trust were asked for input.Belfast TrustBelfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) provided the following table in response to the number of women receiving blood patch treatment for Cerebrospinal Fluid leaks in each of the last five years, per hospital.2017/182018/192019/202020/212021/22Royal Victoria8<5<5<5<5Belfast City<5<5<5<5<5Royal Victoria Maternity<5<5<51510Source: Belfast Health and Social Care TrustWithin these five years, BHSCT indicated that nine women had received more than one blood patch treatment.The frequency and time period between treatments varies from patient to patient, depending on their clinical need and response to the blood patch treatment.South Eastern TrustSouth Eastern Trust responded that not all post-dural puncture headaches require an epidural blood patch, only where it is sufficiently disabling, or where other pain management has failed. On a small number of occasions, a woman may have received a second epidural blood patch where the initial treatment did not alleviate the symptoms, or the initial benefit had subsided. This would usually be performed within a few days of the first patch treatment. For a post-dural puncture headache following obstetric anaesthesia, it is extremely rare for a woman to receive more than two blood patches. Data from the Ulster Hospital from August 2017 – June 2022 is provided below from previous audits, and more recently from database records.From October 2020 to June 2022, there were 1522 epidurals, with 28 documented cases of a post-dural puncture headache diagnosis, and 18 epidural blood patches performed.In 2019, there were 708 epidurals, with 6 accidental dural punctures.From August 2017 until December 2018, there were 1157 epidurals, 19 post-epidural headaches (of which 5 are classified as only ‘possible post-dural puncture headaches’). Five of these were recognised as accidental dural puncture. There were 9 blood patch treatments carried out, and of these, 2 were a second blood patch treatment.Each of the other Health and Social Care Trusts were unable to provide a response in the timeframe provided or only at a disproportionate cost.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what is the accepted best treatment for Cerebrospinal Fluid leakage; and how many women have been given this treatment in each of the last 5 years, broken down by hospital.
answer_text: Treatment for cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) leakage will be determined on the basis of clinical decisions in accordance with an individual patient’s clinical needs.  The services generally available for patients in Northern Ireland with CSF leaks will depend on the cause.  When there has been a procedure related cause, such as those directly related to epidural/spinal anaesthesia during childbirth, or where a diagnostic/therapeutic lumbar puncture has been performed, these will be managed conservatively in the first instance.  In the event that this is unsuccessful, all Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts provide a blood patch service, where this is clinically appropriate.For other types of CSF leak, for example relating to complex spinal or cranial surgery, cases are managed on an individual basis and where clinically appropriate will also be referred outside Northern Ireland in order to receive the appropriate advice and care.Data on the number of women treated for CSF leaks is not collected by my Department, however data on the number of admissions into HSC Trust hospitals, where there was a recorded diagnosis of CSF leak was provided in my response to AQW 1409/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many women have been referred to hospitals in Great Britain or the Republic of Ireland to receive (i) blood patch treatment; and (ii) any other treatment, for Cerebrospinal Fluid leakage.
answer_text: Information on referrals to Great Britain or the Republic of Ireland for treatments of cerebrospinal fluid leakage is not held within my Department. Each of the five Health and Social Care Trusts were approached for this information. South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust provided a response from a consultant anaesthetist working at the Ulster Hospital, since 2001. They stated that they are not aware of any woman from their maternity unit being referred to any other unit, within Northern Ireland or further afield, for the management of an acute cerebrospinal fluid leak following an epidural or spinal procedure on their labour ward.All of the other Health and Social Care Trusts were unable to provide a response in the timeframe provided as data are not held centrally and could only be provided at disproportionate cost.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) when his Department first became aware of cases of Cerebrospinal Fluid leaks in Northern Ireland; and (ii) what action he has taken to establish the extent of the problem.
answer_text: A Cerebrospinal Fluid leak is a medical condition where the cerebrospinal fluid surrounding the brain or spinal cord leaks.  This can be caused by trauma or can occur spontaneously (known as a spontaneous cerebrospinal fluid leak).  There can be many symptoms, diagnostic methods and treatments.  I am therefore unable to determine when my Department first became aware of the existence of this condition.My Department has not been made aware there is a specific issue with the recommended pathway for treatment of cerebrospinal fluid leaks, which I understand to be in line with existing clinical practice and commissioned services.  Where possible, treatment for cerebrospinal fluid leaks are treated within the health and social care service in Northern Ireland.  Patients with complex needs may, however, also be referred outside Northern Ireland in order to receive the appropriate advice and care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what is the (i) average; (ii) shortest; and (iii) longest time taken to diagnose Cerebrospinal Fluid leakage in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Information on time for diagnosis is not held within my Department, and each of the five Health and Social Care Trusts were approached for this information. Belfast Trust indicated that this information could only be provided at disproportionate cost as it is not held centrally. Provision of such information would require a manual trawl of all patient records to establish time between the onset of symptoms to the diagnosis of cerebrospinal fluid leak and subsequent treatment.South Eastern Trust responded that the headache from an accidental dural puncture, caused by the cerebrospinal fluid leakage, may be recognised at the time but the diagnosis of post-dural puncture headache is made on later clinical examination and often does not follow an accidental dural puncture. Onset of headache is not instantaneous, generally symptoms take at least twelve hours to develop, and most often the headache appears 24-48 hours after the spinal or epidural was performed, although in some cases this may be longer than 48hours. Delayed referral may be because the headache was initially mild and only became severe a few days later. At the Ulster Hospital, a computerised database has been able to provide support for this timeline by matching follow-up assessments from the day after women have given birth with the daily assessments taken until they are discharged home.Each of the other Health and Social Care Trusts were unable to provide a response in the timeframe provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many women have required additional treatment as a result of receiving an epidural during childbirth, in each of the last five years, broken down by hospital.
answer_text: The information requested is not held centrally within my Department and each of the Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) were contacted for this information. Four of the five HSCTs contacted have indicated that such information is not readily available. However, Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) has provided the response below:Regarding treatment following epidural, BHSCT anaesthetics team review all patients in the postnatal period for Post-Dural Puncture Headache (PDPH) and any potential neurological symptoms while they are in hospital. The main intervention performed, where necessary, would be an epidural blood patch in PDPH cases when the patient is significantly functionally impaired. Another, less common intervention could involve emergency spinal surgery after epidural abscess/haematoma by the neurosurgical team. However, the rate of this occurring is extremely low, with fewer than five occurrences overall since 2017. Regarding PDPH, and patients requiring epidural blood patch, the table below details the numbers of cases in BHSCT since 2017. All patients receiving epidural blood patch are followed up at the anaesthetic clinic 6 weeks later. question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the steps his Department is taking to identify mitigations to allow existing home care dog boarding providers to operate while legislation is brought forward to update the Animal Boarding Establishments Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1974.
answer_text: It is acknowledged that complying with existing licensing requirements can be difficult for dog home boarding businesses. My Department has not required businesses undertaking this activity to cease trading, but works closely with operators to encourage them to meet licencing conditions where they are able to do so.My Department is committed to reviewing and updating the licensing system for animal boarding establishments, including those that provide boarding from homes, during this mandate. To this end, my officials are presently planning a schedule of animal welfare related legislation to be progressed during this mandate. This will include action on this matter.Please be assured however, that in the meantime, any dogs cared for in a home setting continue to be protected by the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 which provides enforcement agencies with substantial powers to act in situations where an animal is subject to unnecessary suffering in any setting.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the steps his Department is taking to review and update the Animal Boarding Establishments Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1974 to recognise home care dog boarding.
answer_text: My Department is committed to reviewing and updating the licensing system for animal boarding establishments, including those who provide boarding from their homes, during this mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the review of residents' parking schemes; (ii) to detail its findings; and (iii) when it will be published.
answer_text: I can confirm that the review of residents’ parking schemes is complete and I plan to make the report public once I have had the opportunity to discuss its findings with my officials.  The review report will set out the lessons learnt from the Rugby Road scheme in Belfast, and the other schemes that did not make it to implementation, and will be used to establish the policy for such schemes going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what consideration he has given to introducing legislation to regulate estate rent charges and property management companies, to bring them into line with other jurisdictions.
answer_text: The regulation of property management companies is a complex area of law, and provision in relation to this area varies throughout neighbouring jurisdictions. There is no single model with which to align this jurisdiction.Departmental officials have done some further work in this area over recent months and have developed some themes with the intention of potentially seeking views from the public on these issues by way of public consultation. Further work in this area will be subject to the views and priorities of an incoming Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the introduction of Sanctioning for Cyberbullying.
answer_text: When I previously updated you on work to tackle cyber bullying, I also referred to the additional steps I was taking to strengthen the NI legislative framework.  The Protection from Stalking Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 was enacted on 26 April 2022.  It introduces a new offence to address behaviour in a threatening or abusive manner and captures both a single act and a course of conduct that causes fear or alarm, whether conducted in person or online.   This new offence attracts up to 5 years imprisonment and should become operational by the end of summer 2022.  In addition, the Justice (Sexual Offences and Trafficking Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 received Royal Assent on 27 April 2022.   It introduces a number of new offences, which can often be related to online abuse.  These include up-skirting and down-blousing; sending unwanted sexual images (more commonly referred to as cyber-flashing) and offences to deal with adults who masquerade as a child with the aim of sexually grooming children under 16, which can often take place online.  My Department is also working to improve current legislation to support victims of hate crime.  Work has already commenced across a number of the recommendations from Judge Marrinan’s Review of Hate Crime Legislation including dealing with online hate crime through the Online Safety Bill that is being taken forward by the Department of Digital, Culture, Media and Sport (DCMS) as this is a reserved matter.The Online Safety Bill was introduced to Parliament on 17 March 2022 and was debated at second reading on 19 April 2022.  A Public Bill Committee is undertaking line by line scrutiny of the Bill and is expected to report to the House of Commons by 30 June 2022.  My officials continue to work with their DCMS colleagues on the new communications offences included in the Bill; the designation of priority illegal content; proposed repeals of existing communication offences; and more generally on other aspects of the Bill to prevent any gaps in or unintended impact on the Northern Ireland legislative framework.  I have made representations to UKG Ministers about the need to place greater obligations on social media companies to keep people safe.  I will continue to do so as the legislation progresses.I recognise that legislation is not the only way to respond to these issues.  In a previous answer, I also referred to the NI Online Safety Strategy and Action Plan, co-ordinated by the Department of Health but in which all departments have a role.  My department will continue to play its part to help deliver a collective and holistic approach to keeping people safe online as set out in the strategy. The Safeguarding Board for NI has a key coordination role to implement the Strategy.  I am determined to do all I can to help address and prevent bullying and harassment both online and in person.
